name: Ribera del Duero and Armedilla Monastery Loop
name_original: Desde Campasero a Olivares de Duero — circular pasando por Cogoces del Monte
description: This moderate 54.5-mile (87.7 km) road cycling route from Campaspero to Olivares de Duero passes through scenic Valladolid countryside.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Campaspero traverse a landscape characterized by expansive plains and rolling hills within the Castile and León region. The terrain often includes agricultural lands and vineyards, particularly near the Ribera del Duero wine region. Cyclists can expect quiet roads winding through rural environments, offering varied challenges with both ascents and descents. This area provides a blend of open vistas and diverse topography suitable for road cycling. Best road cycling routes around Campaspero The most popular road cycling route is Ribera del Duero and Armedilla Monastery Loop, a 54.5 miles (87.7 km) moderate trail that takes 3 hours 52 minutes to complete. This route features significant elevation changes and passes by historical landmarks. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Cogeces del Monte loop from Cogeces del Monte, an easy 7.1 miles (11.4 km) path. This shorter route offers a gentle ride through the local countryside. Local road cyclists also love the Fuentesaúco de Fuentidueña – Olombrada loop from Campaspero, a 31.8 miles (51.2 km) trail leading through agricultural lands, often completed in about 2 hours 3 minutes. Road cycling around Campaspero is defined by expansive plains, rolling hills, and agricultural landscapes, with routes available for easy, moderate, and challenging rides. The network offers options for different ability levels, from shorter loops to longer, more demanding journeys. The routes in Campaspero are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 2 reviews. More than 90 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Campaspero's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes near Campaspero?
A: The region around Campaspero, situated in Castile and León, offers a diverse road cycling experience. You'll encounter expansive plains, rolling hills, and agricultural lands, including vineyards, especially near the Ribera del Duero wine region. Routes feature both ascents and descents, providing varied challenges on quiet roads through rural environments.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Campaspero?
A: There are 7 road cycling routes available around Campaspero, catering to different skill levels. These include 1 easy route and 6 moderate routes, ensuring options for various cycling preferences.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Campaspero?
A: Yes, beginners can enjoy an easy road cycling route around Campaspero. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38149709" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cogeces del Monte loop from Cogeces del Monte&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 7.1 miles (11.4 km) path, offering a gentle ride through the local countryside.

Q: Are there longer, more challenging road cycling routes available?
A: For those seeking longer and more challenging rides, Campaspero offers several moderate routes. A notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1353852193/ribera-del-duero-and-armedilla-monastery-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ribera del Duero and Armedilla Monastery Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which spans 54.5 miles (87.7 km) and features significant elevation changes, passing by historical landmarks.

Q: What historical landmarks can I see while road cycling near Campaspero?
A: Several routes pass by significant historical landmarks. You can explore the ruins of the 15th-century &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3589685" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Armedilla Monastery&lt;/a&gt;. Additionally, the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7895364" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cuéllar Castle&lt;/a&gt;, with its Gothic and Renaissance architecture, is another prominent attraction in the vicinity.

Q: Are there any notable natural features or viewpoints along the routes?
A: While cycling, you'll experience wide-open vistas across expansive plains and rolling hills. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5789978" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Cubo Windmill&lt;/a&gt; offers a unique man-made landmark that provides a distinct visual point of interest in the landscape.

Q: What do other cyclists say about the road cycling experience in Campaspero?
A: The road cycling routes around Campaspero are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from 2 reviews. Cyclists often appreciate the varied terrain, from quiet rural roads to routes with significant elevation changes, and the opportunity to explore historical sites.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Campaspero?
A: The region experiences warm, dry summers and very cold winters. For road cycling, the spring and autumn months typically offer the most pleasant conditions, with milder temperatures ideal for longer rides. Summers can be quite hot, while winters may present challenging cold conditions.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes around Campaspero?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Campaspero are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38150480" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuentesaúco de Fuentidueña – Olombrada loop from Campaspero&lt;/a&gt; is a 31.8 miles (51.2 km) circular trail through agricultural lands.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through the Ribera del Duero wine region?
A: Yes, the region's proximity to the Ribera del Duero wine region means some routes traverse this area. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1353852193/ribera-del-duero-and-armedilla-monastery-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ribera del Duero and Armedilla Monastery Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, offering cyclists a chance to experience the landscapes associated with this renowned wine-producing area.

Q: Can I find cafes or places to stop along the road cycling routes?
A: While the routes often wind through quiet rural environments, many pass through or near small villages and towns like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5594023" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cogeces del Monte&lt;/a&gt;. These settlements typically offer opportunities for refreshments, such as cafes or small shops, where cyclists can take a break.

Q: Is parking available for cyclists in Campaspero?
A: As a rural area, Campaspero and its surrounding villages generally offer accessible parking options, particularly in town centers or near popular starting points for routes. It's advisable to check specific starting locations for designated parking areas.
tips: Cogeces del Monte is one of these small towns that is located on the border between the two provinces and that is surrounded by agricultural fields with a river branch that reaches it on its western side.

Ruins of what was a monastery of the Order of San Jerónimo dating from the 15th century. Although you can only go around it from the outside, it is beautiful to visit since it gives rise to the imagination of what this sanctuary would have been.
Valley with entertaining shadows and curves
Olivares de Duero is a small town along the Duero Valley on the old trade route between Zaragoza and Valladolid.
Old road that is currently asphalted and that joins the two roads that run on both sides of the Arroyo de Cogeces. A fast descent that leaves you between mountains!

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This circular road cycling route is approximately 87.7 kilometers (54.5 miles) long. It's estimated to take around 3 hours and 53 minutes to complete, though individual times may vary based on fitness and stops.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this route, and is it suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: The route is rated as moderate. It has an elevation gain of about 590 meters (1,936 feet). While manageable for riders with good physical condition, some segments may be unpaved and challenging for a standard road bike. Beginners with good fitness might find it a significant but rewarding challenge.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: Since this is a circular route passing through several towns, you could potentially start and park in Campaspero, Olivares de Duero, or Cogeces del Monte. Look for public parking areas within these villages. Specific parking for cyclists isn't detailed, so general village parking would be your best option.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain and scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: The route takes you through the Valladolid region of Castilla y León, Spain. You can expect rolling hills, rural landscapes, and charming villages. While primarily a road cycling route, be aware that some segments may be unpaved. The broader Duero region is known for historical sites and vineyards, offering views typical of the Castilian countryside.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes by several interesting spots. You'll go through Cogeces del Monte, and near the Armedilla Monastery. Other highlights include the Valimón Valley, the Stone Bridge of Olivares de Duero, and sections of the Camino de la Pipa.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Valladolid region, part of Castilla y León, experiences hot summers and cold winters. Spring (April-May) and Autumn (September-October) generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for cycling, avoiding the extreme heat of summer and the chill of winter.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly overlaps with several other paths. You'll find sections that are part of the Ruta del Duero, Etapa 13: Quintanilla de Onésimo – Tudela de Duero, Sendero de la Armedilla, Etapa 14: Tudela de Duero – Puente Duero, and Sendero del Valcorba.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Generally, no specific permits or entrance fees are required for cycling on public roads and trails in the Valladolid region, including this route. The route is not within a national park with entry fees. Always respect local regulations and private property.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As a road cycling route, it's generally not recommended to bring dogs along, especially for the entire 87.7 km distance. If you do bring a dog, ensure it is safely secured and able to keep pace without risk to itself or others, particularly on public roads. Always check local regulations regarding dogs in public spaces.

faqs.question: Are there places to find food, drinks, or accommodation along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes through or near several villages like Campaspero, Cogeces del Monte, and Olivares de Duero. These towns are likely to have cafes, pubs, and restaurants where you can refuel. Accommodation options may also be available in these settlements if you plan to break up the ride or stay overnight.

faqs.question: What should I pack for a day trip on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: For this moderate road cycling route, essential items include a well-maintained racebike, helmet, appropriate cycling attire, water bottles (enough for the duration, with refill points in villages), snacks, a basic repair kit (spare tube, pump, tire levers), sun protection, and a fully charged phone. Given potential unpaved segments, consider tires suitable for varied surfaces.

waypoints.name: Stone Bridge of Olivares de Duero
